name: Sierra de Líjar — circular from La Muela
name_original: Sierra de Líjar — circular desde La Muela
region_intro: Bus station hiking trails in Sierra Lijar navigate a Jurassic limestone mountain range characterized by rugged landscapes and significant elevation changes. The region features Mediterranean mountain vegetation, including holm oaks, carob trees, and wild olives, with shrubland dominating plateaus. Its karstic nature has created numerous cavities and caves. The Sierra reaches an altitude of 3,415 feet (1041 meters). Best bus station hiking trails in Sierra Lijar The most popular bus station hiking route is Vía Verde de la Sierra – Coripe loop from Coripe, a 6.9 miles (11.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 7 minutes to complete, following a former railway line through varied terrain. Another top favourite among local hikers is El Chaparro de la Vega – Zaframagón Viaduct loop from Coripe, a moderate 8.9 miles (14.4 km) path. This route offers views of the Zaframagón Viaduct and the surrounding karstic landscape. Local hikers also love the Gillete Viaduct – Zaframagón Viaduct loop from Coripe, a 9.4 miles (15.1 km) trail leading through the Sierra's rugged terrain and past viaducts, often completed in about 4 hours 11 minutes. Hiking in Sierra Lijar is defined by limestone formations, Mediterranean forests, and panoramic viewpoints like Mirador de Levante. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to more challenging ascents. The routes in Sierra Lijar are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 800 reviews. More than 4000 hikers have used komoot to explore Sierra Lijar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many bus station hiking trails are available in Sierra Lijar?
A: There are over 25 hiking routes accessible from bus stations in Sierra Lijar. These trails range from easy strolls to more challenging ascents, catering to various fitness levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on bus station hikes in Sierra Lijar?
A: The Sierra de Líjar is characterized by its rugged Jurassic limestone landscape, offering a mix of rocky paths, steep ascents, and panoramic viewpoints. You'll encounter Mediterranean mountain vegetation, including oaks and wild olives, with some routes featuring significant elevation changes.

Q: Are there any easy bus station hikes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, there are a few easier options. While many routes involve moderate to difficult terrain, you can find trails with minimal elevation gain. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31737135" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Majaceite River Trail – Honda Waterfall of Benamahoma loop from Benamahoma&lt;/a&gt; offers a more gentle experience, often recommended for families due to its relatively flat profile.

Q: What are some notable natural features or viewpoints I can discover on these trails?
A: The Sierra de Líjar boasts impressive natural beauty. Many trails lead to stunning vistas, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/328573" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Levante Viewpoint, Sierra de Líjar&lt;/a&gt;, which offers panoramic views of Zahara de la Sierra, its reservoir, and the Sierra de Grazalema Natural Park. On clear days, you might even see Sierra Nevada.

Q: Are there any circular routes starting from bus stations?
A: Yes, several circular routes are accessible from bus stations, allowing you to return to your starting point without needing additional transport. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19321662" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde de la Sierra – Coripe loop from Coripe&lt;/a&gt;, which provides a scenic round trip.

Q: What is the best time of year to hike in Sierra Lijar from a bus station?
A: Spring and autumn are generally the best seasons for hiking in Sierra Lijar. The temperatures are milder, and the landscape is vibrant. Summers can be very hot, making early morning or late afternoon hikes preferable, while winters can bring cooler temperatures but also clear, crisp days.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the bus station hiking trails in Sierra Lijar?
A: Many trails in Sierra Lijar are dog-friendly, but it's always advisable to keep your dog on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or near livestock. Be mindful of the terrain, as some paths can be rocky or steep, and ensure your dog has access to water, particularly on longer routes.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the bus station trails in Sierra Lijar?
A: The komoot community rates the hiking experience in Sierra Lijar highly, with an average score of 4.4 stars. Reviewers often praise the stunning panoramic views, the rugged natural beauty of the limestone mountains, and the sense of tranquility found on these trails.

Q: Are there any routes that feature water elements like rivers or waterfalls?
A: Yes, some routes incorporate water features. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31718731" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Majaceite River Trail – Honda Waterfall of Benamahoma loop from El Bosque&lt;/a&gt; is a notable example, offering a chance to experience the river and potentially see the Honda Waterfall, depending on water levels.

Q: What wildlife might I encounter while hiking in Sierra Lijar?
A: Sierra de Líjar is a haven for wildlife, particularly birds of prey. You might spot vultures, imperial eagles, Egyptian vultures, and Bonelli's eagles soaring above. The numerous caves and sinkholes also provide refuge for bats.

Q: Are there any longer, more challenging bus station hikes for experienced hikers?
A: For experienced hikers seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/21827343" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Chaparro de la Vega – Zaframagón Viaduct loop from Coripe&lt;/a&gt; offer significant distances and elevation gains. These trails require good physical condition and sturdy footwear due to their demanding nature and rocky sections.
tips: Special track to spend time watching the people who practice paragliding. You can see the views of the surrounding mountains such as the Sierra de la Nava or the Río Guadalete valley.
Old walls and spaces that had been part of houses that had been inhabited in the Sierra de Líjar.

The Mirador de Levante is located at the top of the Sierra de Líjar, in Algodonales, Cádiz. This viewpoint offers stunning panoramic views of the natural surroundings, including the town of Zahara de la Sierra and its reservoir, the Sierra de Grazalema Natural Park, and Algodonales itself.
From the viewpoint, you can admire spectacular views of the surrounding landscapes, including the Peña de los Enamorados de Antequera and, on clear days, as far as the Sierra Nevada.
